_source: Curated from the WHO Model List of Essential Medicines (22nd edition, 2021) and WHO Model Formulary guidance. Educational reference only — not a substitute for a clinician or pharmacist.

Respiratory Infection (Flu/COVID).self_care_notes: Rest, fluids, and fever/pain control. Avoid antibiotics unless a clinician confirms a bacterial infection.

who_eml_medications.typical_adult_dose: Chew 300mg immediately if advised by emergency services

Cardiovascular Alert (Angina/Arrhythmia).self_care_notes: This pattern is not self-treatable. Chest pain, arrhythmia, or breathlessness needs immediate emergency evaluation.

who_eml_medications.typical_adult_dose: 400 mg every 6-8h with food, max 1200mg/day OTC

Severe Migraine / Neurological.self_care_notes: Rest in a dark, quiet room. Sudden 'worst-ever' headache, confusion, or vision loss needs urgent care.

who_eml_medications.typical_adult_dose: 20 mg once daily before food, short course

Gastrointestinal Disorder (IBS/Reflux).self_care_notes: Smaller meals, avoid trigger foods. Persistent pain or black stools need a doctor visit.

Metabolic / Diabetes Risk Alert.self_care_notes: This pattern reflects a metabolic risk signal, not a medication-treatable event. Book a routine blood glucose (HbA1c) check with a doctor.

who_eml_medications.typical_adult_dose: Thin layer on affected area, 1-2x daily, short course

Dermatological Infection / Allergic Reac.self_care_notes: Avoid the suspected trigger. Spreading rash, swelling of the face/throat, or breathing difficulty is an emergency.

who_eml_medications.typical_adult_dose: 400 mg every 6-8h with food, max 1200mg/day OTC

Musculoskeletal Strain & Arthritis.self_care_notes: Rest, ice/heat, gentle movement. Numbness, inability to bear weight, or deformity needs urgent evaluation.

Anxiety & Panic Attack.self_care_notes: Grounding/breathing techniques help acute episodes. Because panic attacks can mimic cardiac or respiratory emergencies, first-time or severe episodes should be medically evaluated before assuming anxiety.

who_eml_medications.typical_adult_dose: As per WHO pediatric diarrhoea protocol; check with a pharmacist for children

Viral Gastroenteritis (Stomach Flu).self_care_notes: Hydration is the priority. Blood in stool, high fever, or signs of dehydration need urgent care.

Upper Respiratory Infection (Common Cold.self_care_notes: Rest, fluids, and steam inhalation. Usually self-limiting within 7-10 days.

Chronic Fatigue & Occupational Burnout.self_care_notes: Prioritize sleep, workload reduction, and stress management. Persistent fatigue over weeks warrants a checkup to rule out anemia, thyroid, or metabolic causes.

Acute Dehydration / Electrolyte Alert.self_care_notes: Rehydrate with ORS, not just plain water. Confusion, fainting, or inability to keep fluids down needs urgent care.

Sinusitis / Facial Congestion.self_care_notes: Steam and saline rinses help drainage. Symptoms beyond 10 days or facial swelling need a doctor visit.

Acute Insomnia & Sleep Deprivation.self_care_notes: Sleep hygiene (consistent schedule, reduced screens/caffeine) is the first-line approach. Sedatives are not self-prescribed and require a doctor's judgment.

General Wellness / Normal Health State.self_care_notes: No concerning pattern detected. Keep up the daily check-ins.
